paragraphs.body: As Anne Lamott says in Bird by Bird, “Good dialogue is such a pleasure to come across while reading, a complete change of pace from description and exposition and all that writing.” I’ve come to agree with her over the course of this class. When stories start off with exposition dumping and long-winded descriptions, it can be hard to really get into them. Dialogue is a good way to break that up, and if done right, can help build the story even more. Dialogue can help with characterization, create conflict, and drive stories forward.

One of the first stories assigned for class, “The Apartment” by T. C. Boyle really shows how dialogue is a “complete change of pace,” as Lamott says. The story starts off with three pages of exposition. Boyle introduces the main character by writing about his life up to the point of the story, along with introducing a problem that helps create the main conflict of the story. Boyle does all of this without any dialogue. When the main character meets Madam C., another important character in the story, Boyle finally uses dialogue. Madam C says, “Well, don’t these look pretty?” Because the story up to this point focuses on the main character wanting to buy the apartment from Madam C., having her first point of dialogue be about the flowers breaks up the more serious tone that the beginning of the story has. It helped me as a reader take a step away from the exposition and start to focus on the characters more, especially since the characters have many conversations from this point on.

annotations.comment: I had not really thought about that aspect of the story before, the movement from exposition (although I do think it’s both necessary and well written!) to mostly dialogue. Thank you.

paragraphs.body: Madam C’s line above also helped with her characterization. She knew that the main character was there to buy her apartment, and she chose to talk about the flowers instead. It showed me that she was more than just the little old lady the main character thought she was. Madam C. was having fun with the main character and his proposal. Her dialogue throughout the story showed me that she was smart, funny, and still full of life despite her age.

paragraphs.body: Another short story from The Best American Short Stories of 2020 called “The Nanny” by Emma Cline also shows how dialogue can help with characterization. This story had a side character named Mary that wasn’t that important to the main character of the story, and because of that, I didn’t learn very much about her. However, through the use of dialogue, I was able to figure out more of her characterization. Mary would frequently check up on the main character asking, “You okay?” or “You need anything, sweetie?” This told me that Mary was a kind and caring person who wanted to help the main character as much as she could.

annotations.comment: Yes. And works with the pretty narrow focus of the narrator.

paragraphs.body: After seeing what dialogue can do for a story, I wanted to try it myself in my own writing. Because I didn’t have much practice with dialogue, it fell flat in my first story. I mainly used the dialogue to get certain things, such as the main character’s nickname that was also the title, into the story. Since I didn’t have much experience with dialogue, it came across as a little awkward. I didn’t really use it to create conflict or help give my characters a personality, either. It was mainly filler. After receiving feedback from classmates on my story along with helpful tips from the dialogue chapter of Bird by Bird, I felt like I had learned how to use dialogue better. So, I decided to try it again in my second short story.

annotations.comment: Perhaps, but you tried and that’s really good! Dialogue is hard. Takes a lot of practice to get good at it.

paragraphs.body: I wanted to challenge myself with my second story and really try to use dialogue as much as I could. I ended up writing a story that was almost entirely dialogue. My main focus was making it sound natural, which other than a few exceptions, I think I achieved. I also wanted to use dialogue to create characterization and create conflict, since I had seen other stories do the same thing. The dialogue I used also helped to drive the story without using long paragraphs for exposition.

annotations.context_right: as much as I could. I ended up writing a story that was almost entirely dialogue. My main focus was

paragraphs.body: I wanted my main character of my second story to be self-absorbed and egotistical, which I achieved through her dialogue with other characters. I used multiple conversations to point towards their characterization, and ultimately used the line, “I mean, I’m the best resident here. I can’t be fired. What would the hospital do without me?” to reinforce her personality. I also used a conversation between the main character and a side character to create the conflict of the story, which was that the main character got fired from her job. Because the story was mainly dialogue, any exposition needed came across in conversations, which helped me avoid using long paragraphs to explain things. I was also able to use the dialogue to help drive the story, since all of the scenes contained dialogue.

paragraphs.body: I’ve learned a lot about dialogue in this class. Reading stories using dialogue in different ways, along with Bird by Bird, helped me see how it can be used for more than just simple conversations between characters. And while I have made some improvements, I would like to continue to practice using dialogue and even learn new ways dialogue can impact a story. I believe this will help me to become an even better writer.

annotations.comment: Yep, for sure. Nice paper, Waylon, and thank you for your work this term!
